steps.title: Use the contrast in the blank
steps.content: The passage says crop vocabulary often travels with crops as they spread, then pivots with the idea that crops may also be decoupled from vocabulary. Since corn cultivation became widespread in the Southeast, the blank must show that the related vocabulary did not necessarily spread with it.

steps.title: Reject the wrong data uses
steps.content: Choice B only lists the region's language diversity, which does not address corn vocabulary. Choice C describes vocabulary entering another language and changing, so it supports vocabulary traveling rather than decoupling. Choice D describes shared roots within the Muskogean family, but not whether those words relate to Totozoquean vocabulary.

steps.title: Pick the decoupling evidence
steps.content: The needed evidence is that corn words can have different origins even where corn cultivation is common. Choice A supplies that contrast by saying Cherokee and Muskogean words for corn show no demonstrable relationship to Totozoquean vocabulary. The answer is A.
